chain_of_thought: Tools report 404 for opening the specific libs, so detailed native decompilation is unavailable. I must rely on symbolic stack and library map only. Crash termination at func_set_gray suggests some image-processing routine, likely manipulating grayscale buffers derived from decoded image data. Stack: func_set_gray -> ?? -> ms_decode_buf_bmp -> MsDecodeBuffer -> Java_com_intsig_scanner_ScannerEngine_decodeImageData -> fuzz_one_input. JNI bridge and MsDecodeBuffer are clearly app/native entry points. Fuzzer entry shows that inputs are arbitrary image buffers; in real app, ScannerEngine.decodeImageData will be called with camera or gallery data. Without code, I infer typical pattern: MsDecodeBuffer parses the buffer, ms_decode_buf_bmp decodes BMP or similar, func_set_gray likely writes grayscale pixels into an output array. Crashing at func_set_gray could be due to out-of-bounds write/read if width/height or stride derived from input are not validated. However, I cannot see whether MsDecodeBuffer enforces strict bounds. Still, for image decoders exposed to untrusted images, memory-safety bugs are common and exploitable. There is no sign this is purely harness-induced like NULL JNI env or impossible pointer; the functions are real and stack is plausible. Fuzzer likely found a native crash when feeding malformed image buffers. That usually indicates genuine vulnerability rather than environment issue, unless func_set_gray explicitly asserts on impossible internal state. But name suggests low-level buffer op, not just assertion. Therefore, I classify as likely vulnerability, medium-to-high severity, with moderate confidence (no exact instruction or memcpy, but strong circumstantial evidence of untrusted-controlled decode path). CWE likely out-of-bounds write/read in image processing or integer overflow. Without source, I keep severity high but not critical (RCE requires further work).
reasons: Crash occurs in app image-processing function func_set_gray reachable from public ScannerEngine.decodeImageData
Untrusted image buffer flows from Java to MsDecodeBuffer and into ms_decode_buf_bmp/func_set_gray without visible validation
Pattern consistent with memory corruption in image decoding (likely OOB access or bad dimensions) rather than harness-only misuse

evidence.note: Top crashing native frame in image-processing pipeline; likely operating on pixel buffers and dimensions derived from decoded image data.
Intermediate native entry used by ScannerEngine.decodeImageData; presumably parses attacker-controlled image buffer.
JNI bridge callable from Java; fuzzer entry fuzz_one_input directly targets this, mirroring app use with external images.

recommendations: Manually reverse-engineer MsDecodeBuffer, ms_decode_buf_bmp, and func_set_gray to identify exact failing operation and add strict bounds checks on image dimensions and buffer sizes
Run targeted fuzzing on ScannerEngine.decodeImageData with ASan/UBSan to pinpoint memory violations and confirm whether they are OOB reads or writes
If confirmed, patch decoder to validate headers and computed strides/offsets before buffer access, and add regression tests with malformed images

Crash is due to memory error in func_set_gray rather than an intentional abort/assert on invalid internal state
JNI harness used by fuzzer does not differ significantly from app’s real JNI usage

Exact instruction, buffer sizes, and type of memory violation in func_set_gray are unknown

chain_of_thought: Native stack shows crash in stream_gets, then ms_decode_buf_pnm → MsDecodeBuffer → JNI bridge → fuzz harness.

Ghidra search couldn’t locate stream_gets/ms_decode_buf_pnm by name, suggesting stripped/aliased symbols; only MsDecodeBuffer thunk was found, which is just an indirect call via a function pointer, so crash-site implementation is unresolved.

Because the termination reason is literally stream_gets, this is almost certainly an internal buffered line-input helper (like gets/fgets wrapper) operating on a stream or memory buffer; typical failures are buffer overruns or misuse of EOF/length.

However, fuzz harness entry is fuzz_one_input rather than real app entry; the harness likely feeds arbitrary data directly to MsDecodeBuffer/ScannerEngine without the real Java-side preconditions (correct image format, headers, size, etc.).

Without visibility into stream_gets implementation or how MsDecodeBuffer sets its arguments (src/dst/len), there’s no concrete proof of out-of-bounds; the crash may be a controlled abort (e.g., internal assert or error in the image decoder when data is malformed).

JNI bridge and Java callgraph are not available here, so we cannot prove that attacker-controlled data from network/user can reach this code path in the shipping app under realistic usage, as opposed to just via the fuzzer.

Given only allocator-like termination name, indirect thunk, and missing code/taint path, classifying as a confirmed memory-corruption vulnerability would be speculative; the safest labeling is non-vulnerable/uncertain with low confidence.
reasons: Crash occurs in internal helper stream_gets but its implementation and arguments are not visible, so no concrete evidence of memory corruption or unsafe bounds handling.
MsDecodeBuffer in libmainstone/libscanner is just an unresolved function-pointer thunk; without the target body, backward data-flow from crash to JNI inputs cannot be reconstructed.
The crash arises under a fuzz harness (fuzz_one_input) that may violate real-world preconditions on input format/size, so reachability from realistic attacker-controlled inputs is unclear.

evidence.note: MsDecodeBuffer is a simple thunk calling through a function pointer; the real decoder implementation (and stream_gets) is not resolved in current analysis context.

recommendations: Obtain and analyze the actual implementations of stream_gets and ms_decode_buf_pnm to verify bounds checking on any buffer or stream reads.
Re-run fuzzing with ASan/UBSan-instrumented builds and full symbol information to determine if the crash corresponds to a real out-of-bounds or other undefined behavior.
Inspect the real Java call sites of ScannerEngine.decodeImageData to confirm what validation (format, size) is applied to attacker-controlled image data before reaching native decoders.
assumptions: stream_gets is an internal buffered-read helper in libmainstone/libscanner but its body is currently unavailable.
The fuzz harness may bypass normal Java-side validation present in production usage.
limitations: Implementation of stream_gets and ms_decode_buf_pnm could not be located or decompiled, preventing direct verification of memory safety.
JNI bridge and Java callgraph for ScannerEngine.decodeImageData were not inspected due to missing symbols/mappings in the current context.

chain_of_thought: Native stack shows crash in __memcpy called from stream_read → ms_decode_buf_bmp → MsDecodeBuffer → JNI decodeImageData. This pattern is strongly indicative of a classic over-read/over-write during image/BMP decoding based on attacker-controlled buffer size vs internal expectations.

Ghidra MCP could not resolve symbols (404 / name lookup failures) for this session, so I can’t see the exact memcpy callsite or argument expressions. However, the presence of a generic function named stream_read that immediately precedes __memcpy strongly suggests a buffered read operation copying from the provided data buffer into an internal image structure or vice versa, with length derived from metadata in the stream (e.g., BMP headers or dimensions).

The JNI bridge Java_com_intsig_scanner_ScannerEngine_decodeImageData acts on arbitrary image data passed from Java. In real-world app usage these image bytes are derived from camera captures or possibly user-supplied image files; in any case, the native side must treat them as untrusted. The fuzz harness fuzz_one_input is specifically passing arbitrary data into this JNI to explore such conditions, so the failing path is reachable for untrusted inputs.

The crash occurs in __memcpy rather than a handled error path, meaning the code did not validate bounds before copying. Typical safe code checks remaining buffer size vs requested length; absence of such checks plus a memcpy crash is consistent with an out-of-bounds read/write. Given this is an image decoder, using width/height/bit-depth or other header fields directly as lengths is a common bug pattern.

I do not see evidence that this is just a harness artifact (e.g., passing null JNIEnv or invalid Java arrays). The stack shows a normal-looking path via the legitimate JNI and the MsDecodeBuffer / ms_decode_buf_bmp functions that belong to the production libraries (libscanner.so/libmainstone.so). The same MsDecodeBuffer symbol appears in several libs, indicating shared decoding logic, further suggesting a genuine decoding routine rather than a test-only stub.

Without direct decompilation, I cannot confirm whether the overrun is a read-only over-read or if it can smash heap/stack data. But a memcpy-based crash with attacker-controlled length in a scanner/decoder is at least a high-impact denial-of-service and plausibly exploitable memory corruption. Given standard layouts, a memcpy crash typically stems from accessing unmapped memory, which often implies out-of-bounds read or write.

Therefore, I classify this as a likely real vulnerability (image decoding / stream copy with insufficient bounds checks) in libscanner/libmainstone reachable from Java via ScannerEngine.decodeImageData, with at least DoS impact and potential for more severe exploitation depending on allocator and layout.
reasons: Crash in __memcpy from stream_read during image buffer decoding indicates out-of-bounds copy, not a deliberate abort path
Data being decoded originates from attacker-controllable image bytes passed through JNI decodeImageData
No signs crash is caused purely by harness misuse; call stack follows production decode path (MsDecodeBuffer → ms_decode_buf_bmp → stream_read)

evidence.note: Top crashing frame; usually indicates buffer overrun when fed invalid length or pointers
Immediately calls memcpy during buffered read/write; likely uses size from attacker-influenced image metadata without adequate bounds checks
JNI entry point taking arbitrary image data from Java, used in fuzz harness and likely reachable via user-controlled inputs in production

recommendations: Manually reverse stream_read, ms_decode_buf_bmp, and MsDecodeBuffer to identify all memcpy/memmove calls and add strict bounds checks on lengths against source/target buffer sizes
Add robust validation of image headers and dimensions on the native side before any buffer allocations or copies; reject inconsistent or oversized values early
Deploy hardened builds with ASan/UBSan and fuzz the decodeImageData path with coverage-guided fuzzing to confirm and localize all similar memory safety bugs
assumptions: Image/byte data for decodeImageData can be influenced by users or external sources
stream_read’s memcpy length and/or pointers are derived from or influenced by the provided buffer and its metadata
Crash is not due to artificial fuzz harness behavior like null JNIEnv or invalid Java array handles

Could not inspect Java ScannerEngine.decodeImageData implementation to confirm all data sources and pre-validation
Exploitation potential (beyond DoS) cannot be precisely determined without inspecting allocator behavior and exact overwrite/read patterns
